title: Ful medames
subtitle: Egyptian stewed fava beans with lemon and cumin
excerpt: Dried fava beans soaked overnight and slow-cooked until completely soft, then mashed roughly and finished with lemon juice, cumin, garlic, and olive oil. One of the oldest continuously eaten dishes in the world, and still the most common breakfast in Egypt and across much of the Arab world. Served with hard-boiled eggs, pitta, and fresh vegetables.
sourceNotes: Ful medames is Egypt's national dish and the most widely eaten breakfast in the Arab world. Claudia Roden's 'A Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1968) documents its appearance across Egypt, Syria, Lebanon, Jordan, and Sudan. The dish has been traced to ancient Egypt, with records of fava bean cultivation along the Nile dating to at least 2500 BCE. The modern form, cooked in a large pot overnight and sold by street vendors at dawn, has been a Cairo institution for centuries.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooked beans for up to 3 months. Defrost and reheat gently with a splash of water.
recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the quantity — ful improves over days and keeps in the fridge for 4 days.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the beans ahead and refrigerate. Reheat and add the lemon, garlic, and olive oil just before serving.

content.text: The beans must be completely soft before seasoning: they should be breaking down and yielding when pressed. Undercooked ful is grainy and dense. The traditional method is overnight slow cooking in a sealed pot; at home, a rolling simmer for 1 to 1.5 hours achieves the same result. Dried small fava beans (sold in Middle Eastern shops) work best; larger ones take longer and have thicker skins. Some recipes call for peeling the skins, but leaving them on adds texture.

The finishing is everything. The beans themselves are bland — the lemon, raw garlic, and good olive oil stirred in at the end transform them into something sharp and alive. Season assertively. Serve in bowls drizzled with more olive oil, with hard-boiled eggs on the side, warm pitta, sliced tomato, and cucumber.

items.prepNote: soaked overnight in cold water, drained

content.text: Drain the soaked {{fava-beans-dried}} and place in a large saucepan. Cover with plenty of cold water — the beans will absorb a large amount. Bring to the boil, skim off any foam, then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook for 1 to 1.5 hours until the beans are very soft and some are beginning to collapse. Add boiling water during cooking if the level drops below the beans. Do not add salt until the end: salting too early toughens the skins.

Drain the beans, reserving some cooking liquid. Return to the pan. Mash roughly with a fork or the back of a spoon, leaving about half the beans whole for texture. Stir in the {{garlic}}, {{lemon}} juice, {{cumin-ground}}, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, and salt. Add a splash of cooking liquid if too thick. Taste and adjust — the ful should be deeply savoury, lemony, and fragrant with cumin. Serve in bowls topped with {{parsley-flat}}, a drizzle of extra olive oil, and {{chilli-flakes}} if using. Place the hard-boiled {{eggs}} alongside.

items.symptom: Beans are still firm after 1.5 hours
items.cause: Old dried beans or not soaked long enough
items.fix: Dried legumes can take longer if old. Soak for a full 12 hours. Continue cooking, checking every 15 minutes. Very old beans can take 2 hours or more

items.cause: Not enough lemon or garlic, or garlic not raw
items.fix: Raw garlic is essential — cooked garlic produces a very different result. Add the garlic at the end with the lemon. Taste repeatedly and add lemon until the flavour is bright

items.symptom: Beans are watery rather than thick
items.cause: Too much water in the pot at the end
items.fix: Drain excess cooking liquid before seasoning. The consistency should be porridge-like — spoonable but not souplike. Mash more beans to thicken

items.cause: Old beans or cooking time too short
items.fix: The skins of small fava beans are usually fine. Larger beans may need their skins slipped off after cooking — pinch each bean and the skin should slide free when fully cooked

content.text: Ful medames is the most important breakfast food in Egypt and one of the defining dishes of Arab food culture. In Cairo it is sold by street vendors from large copper pots that have been cooking through the night, ladled out at dawn to workers, students, and anyone passing. The name 'medames' means 'buried' or 'buried underground' in Coptic, referring to the ancient practice of slow-cooking the beans in clay pots in the embers of cooling bread ovens. Ful crosses religious and class lines in Egypt — it is eaten by everyone, rich or poor, Muslim or Christian, as a morning meal. The versions vary by region and family: some are almost smooth, some chunky, some finished with tomato or tahini or hot sauce. The constant is the fava bean, lemon, and cumin.
